question: Create an `Employee` class with `name` and `id`. Design a `Team` class that manages an array of `Employee` pointers to represent team members. Demonstrate adding employees to the team without the team owning their memory.

test_cases.expected:
Added employee Alice to team.
Added employee Bob to team.
Added employee Charlie to team.
Added employee David to team.

--- Team Members ---
Employee ID: 101, Name: Alice
Employee ID: 102, Name: Bob
Employee ID: 103, Name: Charlie
Employee ID: 104, Name: David
--------------------
(Note: This output assumes the program doesn't crash immediately due to buffer overflow, but the issue is present. In a real scenario, adding David would write past the end of the `teamMembers` array, leading to undefined behavior or a crash.)

errors: The `Team::addEmployee` method uses a fixed-size raw array (`teamMembers`) but fails to check if the array is full before attempting to add a new employee. This can lead to a buffer overflow if more than `MAX_TEAM_MEMBERS` employees are added, resulting in undefined behavior or a program crash.
The `Team` class uses a C-style raw array instead of `std::vector`, which is more prone to such buffer overflow errors and requires manual size management.
